question_title: Will the interest in “the visit” change between 2026-03-31 and 2026-04-10 according to Google Trends?
summary: Google Trends interest in "the visit" (2015 horror movie) starts at 66 on Mar 31, post-spike from a Mar 25 peak of 100. The key dynamics are: (1) mean-reversion pressure toward the pre-spike baseline of ~55-59, favoring Decreases; (2) high spike frequency (~2 per month from Netflix recommendation cycles), giving ~25-49% probability of another spike in the 10-day window, favoring Increases; and (3) the ±3 "Doesn't change" band [63, 69] being very narrow relative to the high daily volatility (std ~7-13 points), making DC unlikely. Monte Carlo simulations across multiple models consistently yield ~35-50% Increases, 7-15% DC, 38-60% Decreases. Mean reversion slightly dominates, but spike upside makes Increases competitive.

factors.description: Mean reversion toward pre-spike baseline (~59): current value 66 is elevated post-spike (Mar 25), pre-spike values were 52-59. Expected drift of ~-3.5 over 10 days pushes end value toward 62-63, in Decreases territory.
Trailing change stats show decay: last 6 daily transitions show 50% decrease rate vs 17% increase rate, consistent with ongoing post-spike decay.
High spike frequency: two spikes in 30 days (Feb 27, Mar 25) = ~1 per 15 days. P(spike in 10-day window) ≈ 25-49%. If spike lands in days 6-10, end value likely >69.
Movie on Netflix: related queries all about 'the visit movie', 'netflix', 'where to watch' - suggesting algorithmic recommendation cycles drive periodic spikes.
Narrow ±3 band vs high volatility: daily std ~7-13 points, 10-day P10-P90 spans ~46 points. The 7-point DC window [63-69] is very narrow, consistently yielding 7-15% DC in simulations.
SerpAPI measurement uncertainty: ±1-2 point difference between pytrends and SerpAPI could shift borderline outcomes. Slightly expands effective DC probability.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary:**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| **Decreases** | **45%** |
| **Increases** | **40%** |
| **Doesn't change** | **15%** |

**Key reasoning:**

- **Start value: 66** (Mar 31), within the resolution window scaled to max 100 (Mar 25 spike)
- **Mean reversion dominates**: Pre-spike baseline was ~52-59. The current 66 is still elevated, with expected drift toward ~62-63 over 10 days, just crossing into Decreases territory
- **Spike risk is substantial**: Two spikes in 30 days suggest ~25-49% chance of another within the window. A late-window spike would push the Apr 10 value well above 69 (Increases)
- **"Doesn't change" is constrained**: The ±3 band [63, 69] is only 7 points wide against daily volatility of 7-13 points. All Monte Carlo models consistently put DC at 7-15%
- The search term is driven by the 2015 M. Night Shyamalan movie cycling through Netflix recommendations, explaining the periodic spike pattern
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=the%20visit&date=2026-03-11%202026-04-10](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=the%20visit&date=2026-03-11%202026-04-10) for 2026-04-10 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-31. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "the visit",
  "date": "2026-03-11 2026-04-10",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
